cards.back: A lookup table of function pointers used to resolve virtual function calls at runtime (virtual table)
A hidden pointer member added to each object of a class with virtual functions, pointing to the class's vtable (virtual pointer)

cards.text: The compiler creates {{c1::one vtable}} per class that has virtual functions, not one per object.

cards.back: An array of function pointers, one for each virtual function accessible by the class (including inherited virtual functions).

cards.text: Each object of a class with virtual functions contains a hidden {{c1::vptr}} (virtual pointer) that points to the vtable for that object's {{c2::class type}}.

cards.concept: How virtual function calls are resolved using vtables
cards.explanation:
When a virtual function is called through a pointer/reference:
  1. Dereference the object's vptr to get the vtable
  2. Look up the function pointer in the vtable (at a fixed offset)
  3. Dereference the function pointer and call the function
This happens at runtime, enabling polymorphism.

cards.front: What is the memory overhead of virtual functions?
cards.back: One vptr (pointer-sized, typically 4 or 8 bytes) per object with virtual functions, plus one vtable (array of function pointers) per class.

cards.text: Calling a virtual function requires {{c1::two}} extra indirections: one to access the vtable through the vptr, and one to call the function through the function pointer.

cards.front: How does a derived class's vtable differ from its base class's vtable?
cards.back:
The derived class's vtable contains:
  • Function pointers to overridden functions (pointing to derived versions)
  • Function pointers to inherited virtual functions not overridden (pointing to base versions)
  • Pointers to any new virtual functions in the derived class

cards.question: How much extra memory does each Dog object use if Dog has virtual functions?
cards.answer: One pointer (vptr), typically 4-8 bytes, in addition to the 12 bytes for age and weight.
cards.explanation: Each object with virtual functions gets a hidden vptr member. The vtable itself is shared among all Dog objects, so it doesn't add per-object overhead.

cards.front: When is the vptr initialized for an object?
cards.back: The vptr is set by the constructor when the object is created. Each constructor in the inheritance hierarchy sets the vptr to point to its class's vtable.

cards.concept: Why virtual functions can't be called from constructors effectively
cards.explanation:
During construction, the vptr points to the current constructor's class vtable, not the most-derived class.

When Base constructor runs:
  • Derived part doesn't exist yet
  • vptr points to Base's vtable
  • Virtual calls resolve to Base versions
Only after construction completes does vptr point to the final derived class vtable.
examples: // Even if Derived d; the Base constructor calls Base version
